content.text: PACT Contracts Between GenAI Service Consumers

Consumer-driven contract testing (CDC) with PACT addresses a specific problem in GenAI microservices: when the API gateway calls the AI inference service, neither team knows whether a schema change by the provider will break the consumer — until it already has. PACT inverts this by having the consumer define what it expects, publishing that expectation as a contract to a broker, and requiring the provider to verify the contract before any deployment.

For GenAI services, PACT covers the API boundary — request schema, response structure, HTTP status codes, and field types. It does not cover output quality or model behaviour. That is the job of the eval suite. The two tools are complementary: PACT ensures the schema is right; evals ensure the content is right.

attrs.alt: PACT flow: consumer writes contract → publishes to broker → provider verifies schema against mocked LLM stub

content.text: Why CDC Contracts Matter for GenAI Service Meshes

A GenAI application typically composes several services: a prompt service, an AI inference service, a retrieval service, and an orchestration layer. Each service has an API that other services depend on. Without contracts, breaking changes propagate silently until a consuming service crashes in staging.
Service
Consumers
Schema at Risk
AI Inference
API Gateway, Orchestrator
Response body structure, citation format, streaming event schema
Retrieval
AI Inference, Orchestrator
Chunk schema, doc_id format, score field presence
Prompt Service
AI Inference
Prompt template format, variable names, version header
Eval Judge
CI eval runner
Score schema, criteria_results structure, verdict field
Writing a PACT Consumer Test
The consumer test runs against a PACT mock server, not the real provider. The mock server records the expected request and response into a PACT contract file.
Like()
and
EachLike()
matchers tell PACT to check type compatibility, not exact value equality — critical for AI services where response text varies:

# tests/contract/consumer/test_api_gateway_consumes_ai_inference.py
"""
Consumer: api-gateway
Provider: ai-inference
Contract: pacts/api-gateway-ai-inference.json (generated here, published to broker)
"""
import pytest
from pact import Consumer, Provider, Like, EachLike, Term
from agents.ai_client import AIInferenceClient  # the consumer's HTTP client

PACT_MOCK_HOST = "localhost"
PACT_MOCK_PORT = 1234

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def pact():
    p = Consumer("api-gateway").has_pact_with(
        Provider("ai-inference"),
        host_name=PACT_MOCK_HOST,
        port=PACT_MOCK_PORT,
        pact_dir="./pacts",
        log_dir="./pact-logs",
    )
    p.start_service()
    yield p
    p.stop_service()


class TestAPIGatewayConsumesAIInference:

    def test_summarise_returns_expected_schema(self, pact):
        expected_body = {
            "summary": Like("any non-empty string"),
            "citations": EachLike({
                "doc_id": Like("doc-123"),
                "title":  Like("Document Title"),
                "score":  Like(0.87),
            }),
            "latency_ms": Like(450),
            "model": Like("gpt-4o-2024-11-20"),
        }

        (pact
            .given("AI inference service is healthy")
            .upon_receiving("a summarise request with query and context")
            .with_request(
                method="POST",
                path="/v1/summarise",
                headers={"Content-Type": "application/json",
                         "Authorization": Term(r"Bearer .+", "Bearer test-token")},
                body={
                    "query":   Like("What is RAG?"),
                    "context": Like("RAG stands for retrieval-augmented generation."),
                    "options": {"max_tokens": Like(512)},
                },
            )
            .will_respond_with(
                status=200,
                headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
                body=expected_body,
            )
        )

        with pact:
            client = AIInferenceClient(base_url=f"http://{PACT_MOCK_HOST}:{PACT_MOCK_PORT}")
            result = client.summarise(
                query="What is RAG?",
                context="RAG stands for retrieval-augmented generation.",
                options={"max_tokens": 512},
                token="test-token",
            )

        assert result["summary"]
        assert isinstance(result["citations"], list)

    def test_summarise_returns_400_for_missing_query(self, pact):
        (pact
            .given("AI inference service is healthy")
            .upon_receiving("a summarise request missing the query field")
            .with_request(
                method="POST",
                path="/v1/summarise",
                headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
                body={"context": "Some context text"},
            )
            .will_respond_with(
                status=400,
                body={"error": Like("query is required"), "code": Like("MISSING_FIELD")},
            )
        )

        with pact:
            client = AIInferenceClient(base_url=f"http://{PACT_MOCK_HOST}:{PACT_MOCK_PORT}")
            response = client.summarise_raw(body={"context": "Some context text"}, token="test-token")

        assert response.status_code == 400
Provider Verification with a Mocked LLM

Provider verification replays each interaction from the contract against the real provider service. The LLM is replaced with a deterministic stub that returns fixed text — the goal is to verify API schema, not output quality. Run verification in CI on every provider commit, and publish results back to the broker so consumers can see whether the provider is safe to deploy against:

# tests/contract/provider/test_ai_inference_provider.py
"""
Provider: ai-inference
Verifies contracts published to the PACT broker by all consumers.
LLM is stubbed — this test covers schema only.
"""
import os, pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from pact import Verifier

PACT_BROKER_URL = os.environ.get("PACT_BROKER_URL", "http://pact-broker:9292")
PROVIDER_URL    = os.environ.get("PROVIDER_URL",    "http://localhost:8080")

# State handlers: set up any provider state named in the consumer tests
STATE_HANDLERS = {
    "AI inference service is healthy": lambda: None,  # default state — no setup needed
    "AI inference service is rate-limited": _setup_rate_limit_state,
}

def _setup_rate_limit_state():
    """Configure the local test server to return 429 for the next call."""    import httpx
    httpx.post(f"{PROVIDER_URL}/_test/force_rate_limit")


# LLM stub: returns a fixed response regardless of prompt
STUB_LLM_RESPONSE = {
    "summary": "This is a stub summary for contract testing.",
    "citations": [{"doc_id": "doc-1", "title": "Test Doc", "score": 0.92}],
    "latency_ms": 100,
    "model": "gpt-4o-2024-11-20",
}

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True, scope="module")
def stub_llm():
    with patch("ai_inference.llm_client.LLMClient.complete",
               return_value=STUB_LLM_RESPONSE):
        yield


def test_provider_honours_all_consumer_contracts():
    verifier = Verifier(
        provider="ai-inference",
        provider_base_url=PROVIDER_URL,
    )
    output, _ = verifier.verify_with_broker(
        broker_url=PACT_BROKER_URL,
        broker_username=os.environ.get("PACT_BROKER_USERNAME"),
        broker_password=os.environ.get("PACT_BROKER_PASSWORD"),
        publish_verification_results=True,
        provider_version=os.environ.get("GIT_COMMIT", "local"),
        provider_states_setup_url=f"{PROVIDER_URL}/_pact/provider_states",
        enable_pending=True,   # pending contracts don't break the build
    )
    assert output == 0, "Provider verification failed — see PACT logs for details"
Publishing Contracts in CI

Add a contract publishing step to the consumer's CI pipeline, immediately after the consumer contract tests pass. The broker records the consumer version so the provider knows which consumer versions it must satisfy:

# .github/workflows/consumer-contracts.yml
name: Consumer Contract Tests
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  contract-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"

      - name: Run consumer contract tests
        run: pytest tests/contract/consumer/ -q
        # Generates ./pacts/api-gateway-ai-inference.json

      - name: Publish PACT contracts to broker
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        env:
          PACT_BROKER_URL: ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_URL }}
          PACT_BROKER_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          pact-broker publish ./pacts \
            --consumer-app-version=${{ github.sha }} \
            --branch=${{ github.ref_name }} \
            --broker-base-url=$PACT_BROKER_URL \
            --broker-token=$PACT_BROKER_TOKEN

      - name: Can-I-Deploy check
        # Confirm the consumer can be deployed against the current provider in prod
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        env:
          PACT_BROKER_URL: ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_URL }}
          PACT_BROKER_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          pact-broker can-i-deploy \
            --pacticipant api-gateway \
            --version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --to-environment production \
            --broker-base-url=$PACT_BROKER_URL \
            --broker-token=$PACT_BROKER_TOKEN
What PACT Contracts Do Not Cover

PACT is a schema and protocol testing tool. It verifies that the API contract is respected — field presence, types, status codes. Three categories of GenAI-specific quality concerns fall entirely outside PACT's scope and must be handled by the eval suite and monitoring layer:
Output quality:
Whether the summary is accurate, grounded, or coherent. PACT checks that the summary field is a non-empty string; the eval judge checks whether it is a good answer.
Prompt-to-output alignment:
Whether the AI model understood the prompt and context correctly. PACT mocks the LLM; it never tests the real model path.
Streaming contract:
Server-sent events (SSE) and chunked streaming are not well-supported in standard PACT matchers. Use integration tests to verify streaming correctness.
Latency SLOs:
PACT does not assert on response time. Latency contracts belong in integration tests or SLO monitoring.
Rate limiting and quota behaviour:
PACT can verify the 429 response schema but cannot reproduce real rate limit conditions. Use a dedicated load test for this.
Handling Streaming APIs in Contract Tests

Server-sent events (SSE) and chunked transfer streaming are the delivery mechanism for most production AI response APIs, but PACT's HTTP contract model assumes a single request/response pair. The standard workaround is to contract the
non-streaming

endpoint (which returns the full JSON at once) for schema verification, and to use a separate integration test for the streaming endpoint. Alternatively, write a thin REST-to-streaming adapter layer whose non-streaming path PACT can test:

# Consumer contract for the non-streaming summarise endpoint
# The streaming /v1/summarise/stream endpoint is verified by integration tests,
# not by PACT, because PACT cannot inspect SSE chunk sequences.

# Pattern: contract the synchronous fallback endpoint
(pact
    .given("summarise endpoint is available in sync mode")
    .upon_receiving("a synchronous summarise request")
    .with_request(
        method="POST",
        path="/v1/summarise",   # sync endpoint — full JSON response
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        body={"query": Like("What is RAG?"), "context": Like("some context"),
              "stream": False},   # explicit sync flag
    )
    .will_respond_with(
        status=200,
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},  # NOT text/event-stream
        body={
            "summary": Like("some text"),
            "citations": EachLike({"doc_id": Like("id")}),
            "usage": {"prompt_tokens": Like(100), "completion_tokens": Like(80)},
        },
    )
)

# For the streaming endpoint, write an integration test that:
# 1. Opens an SSE connection to /v1/summarise/stream
# 2. Collects all "data: {...}" events
# 3. Verifies the concatenated text is non-empty
# 4. Verifies the final [DONE] event is sent
# 5. Verifies total latency-to-first-token < 3s
#
# tests/integration/test_streaming_endpoint.py
import httpx, json, pytest, time

@pytest.mark.integration
async def test_streaming_response_structure():
    chunks = []
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        start = time.monotonic()
        async with client.stream("POST", f"{PROVIDER_URL}/v1/summarise/stream",
                                 json={"query": "What is RAG?", "context": "ctx..."}) as r:
            first_chunk_time = None
            async for line in r.aiter_lines():
                if line.startswith("data: "):
                    payload = json.loads(line[6:])
                    if payload.get("done"):
                        break
                    if first_chunk_time is None:
                        first_chunk_time = time.monotonic() - start
                    chunks.append(payload.get("delta", ""))

    assert chunks, "No chunks received"
    assert "".join(chunks).strip(), "Concatenated stream is empty"
    assert first_chunk_time < 3.0, f"Time to first token: {first_chunk_time:.1f}s"
Contract Versioning and the Provider Compatibility Matrix
As the AI inference service evolves, it must stay compatible with every consumer version that is currently deployed. The PACT broker maintains a
compatibility matrix
— a table of consumer versions × provider versions × verification results. The
can-i-deploy
command queries this matrix before any deployment. Configure the broker to enforce this check in every provider CI pipeline:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/check_provider_compatibility.py
"""
Run before deploying the ai-inference provider to any environment.
Queries the PACT broker compatibility matrix and fails if any consumer
version currently deployed to that environment would break.
"""
import os, subprocess, sys

BROKER_URL   = os.environ["PACT_BROKER_URL"]
BROKER_TOKEN = os.environ["PACT_BROKER_TOKEN"]
PROVIDER     = "ai-inference"
VERSION      = os.environ["GIT_COMMIT"]
ENVIRONMENT  = os.environ.get("DEPLOY_ENV", "production")

def can_i_deploy() -> bool:
    result = subprocess.run([
        "pact-broker", "can-i-deploy",
        "--pacticipant", PROVIDER,
        "--version",     VERSION,
        "--to-environment", ENVIRONMENT,
        "--broker-base-url", BROKER_URL,
        "--broker-token", BROKER_TOKEN,
        "--output", "json",
    ], capture_output=True, text=True)

    print(result.stdout)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print("COMPATIBILITY MATRIX VIOLATION — deploy blocked:")
        print(result.stderr)
        return False
    return True

# Versioning strategy for contract files:
# Consumer version = git SHA (from github.sha in CI)
# Provider version = git SHA (from github.sha in CI)
# Environments recorded in broker: development, staging, production
# Use pact-broker record-deployment after every successful deploy:
#
#   pact-broker record-deployment \
#     --pacticipant ai-inference \
#     --version $GIT_COMMIT \
#     --environment production \
#     --broker-base-url $BROKER_URL \
#     --broker-token $BROKER_TOKEN
#
# This tells the broker which version is live, so can-i-deploy
# checks the right consumer versions.

if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(0 if can_i_deploy() else 1)
Provider State Handlers for AI-Specific Scenarios
Consumer tests reference
provider states
"AI inference service is rate-limited"

) to define the precondition for each interaction. The provider must implement a state handler endpoint that sets up that precondition before each replayed interaction. For GenAI services, common states include model unavailability, rate limiting, and context length overflow:

# ai_inference/test_support/provider_states.py
# Mounted at /_pact/provider_states during provider verification only
from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from unittest.mock import patch
import threading

router = APIRouter()
_active_patches: list = []
_lock = threading.Lock()

class ProviderStateRequest(BaseModel):
    state: str
    params: dict = {}

@router.post("/_pact/provider_states")
async def set_provider_state(req: ProviderStateRequest):
    """Called by the PACT verifier before each interaction replay."""
    global _active_patches
    with _lock:
        # Undo any previous state
        for p in _active_patches:
            p.stop()
        _active_patches = []

        if req.state == "AI inference service is healthy":
            pass  # default — no patch needed

        elif req.state == "AI inference service is rate-limited":
            import ai_inference.llm_client as lc
            p = patch.object(lc.LLMClient, "complete",
                             side_effect=lc.RateLimitError("rate limit exceeded"))
            p.start()
            _active_patches.append(p)

        elif req.state == "model is temporarily unavailable":
            import ai_inference.llm_client as lc
            p = patch.object(lc.LLMClient, "complete",
                             side_effect=lc.ModelUnavailableError("503 from upstream"))
            p.start()
            _active_patches.append(p)

        elif req.state == "context exceeds model token limit":
            import ai_inference.llm_client as lc
            p = patch.object(lc.LLMClient, "complete",
                             side_effect=lc.ContextLengthError("context too long"))
            p.start()
            _active_patches.append(p)

        else:
            raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=f"Unknown state: {req.state!r}")

    return {"state": req.state, "result": "set"}


# Consumer test using these states:
# (pact
#     .given("model is temporarily unavailable")
#     .upon_receiving("a summarise request when model is down")
#     .with_request("POST", "/v1/summarise", body={...})
#     .will_respond_with(
#         status=503,
#         body={"error": Like("model unavailable"), "retry_after_ms": Like(5000)},
#     )
# )
Summary

PACT consumer-driven contract tests protect the API boundary between GenAI microservices — specifically the request schema, response structure, HTTP status codes, and field types. The consumer defines what it expects using
Like()
and
EachLike()
matchers that tolerate text variance, the contract is published to a broker, and the provider verifies it against a mocked LLM stub on every commit.

PACT integrates with the broader test pyramid by occupying the boundary between unit/snapshot tests and integration tests. It runs fast (mocked LLM, no eval suite), provides clear failure messages (schema mismatch vs expected contract), and prevents the silent API breakages that appear when AI service teams evolve their endpoints independently. The
can-i-deploy
check completes the loop: no consumer can deploy to production unless the provider has verified the contract it will rely on.
